Taylor, relies heavily on proxy data from advanced producer service firms, which captures formal corporate connections but may miss broader economic and demographic flows. There is a critical need for a globally consistent, high-frequency, and empirically direct measure of urban connectivity that can validate and complement existing network models. Nighttime light (NTL) intensity, as a correlate of human economic activity and settlement, offers a unique, spatially explicit data source to address this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatio-temporal intensity and co-variation of nighttime lights between city-regions serve as a robust empirical proxy for the functional connectivity of the world-city network. We hypothesize that the derived NTL-based network will show significant correlation with established GaWC-style networks but will reveal distinct patterns, particularly highlighting the intensity of connections between major urban regions in the Global South.
proposals.method: We will utilize the Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS) Day/Night Band monthly composites (2012-2025) at a 15 arc-second resolution. For a defined set of 300 global city-regions, we will extract annual average radiance values. Connectivity between city-pairs (i, j) will be calculated using a gravity model adapted for NTL: Connectivity_ij = (NTL_i * NTL_j) / (Distance_ij^β), where β is a friction parameter calibrated against known flows. We will also employ time-series correlation analysis of NTL fluctuations between cities to infer synchronous economic activity. The resulting adjacency matrix will be analyzed using social network analysis (SNA) metrics (e.g., degree, betweenness centrality) in UCINET.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce the first global, longitudinal map of inter-city connectivity derived directly from a physical signal of human activity, providing an empirical benchmark for world-city network research. It will challenge and refine theoretical models by revealing connections potentially underestimated by firm-based data, particularly within and between emerging economies. The method establishes a reproducible, objective framework for monitoring the dynamic evolution of the urban world-system.
proposals.novelty: While NTL data is used for measuring intra-urban growth and economic activity, its systematic application to model the *inter-urban network* as a dynamic system is novel. This approach moves beyond using NTL as a simple indicator of city size to operationalize it as a direct measure of relational intensity between cities, creating a new empirical lineage in urban network science.

proposals.title: Modeling Global Flows: An Empirical Test of Networked versus Bounded Spatial Logics
proposals.motivation: A central debate in urban and globalization studies concerns the relative importance of city networks versus state territories in structuring global integration. Pre-2026 literature, including Taylor's own work on world cities, posits a networked logic, but empirical comparisons with state-centric explanations remain largely theoretical. This research addresses this gap by providing a rigorous, data-driven test of these competing spatial frameworks.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial distribution of key global flows (e.g., air passenger traffic, venture capital) is better predicted by variables capturing inter-city network connectivity than by variables describing inter-state political and institutional ties.
proposals.method: We adapt the framework of Species Distribution Modeling (SDM). For a target flow (e.g., dyadic air passenger volume), we construct two competing sets of predictor variables: a 'city-network' set (e.g., corporate co-location from GaWC data, airline route frequency, internet backbone capacity) and a 'state-centric' set (e.g., shared trade bloc membership, visa waiver agreements, contiguity). Using pre-2025 data, we will train and compare the predictive performance of two Random Forest regression models—one for each spatial logic—using k-fold cross-validation and testing on held-out temporal data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, quantitative evidence adjudicating between network and territorial logics in global urban studies. It will advance methodological innovation by successfully importing a robust comparative modeling technique from ecology. The findings will clarify the fundamental spatial units of analysis for understanding 21st-century globalization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, empirical competition of frameworks using an adapted ecological methodology, moving beyond descriptive network analysis or state-centric econometrics to a hypothesis-testing model comparison.

proposals.title: Beyond Radiance: Tracking the Functional Typology and Network Evolution of Urban Clusters Using Nighttime Lights and Infrastructure Data
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on world cities and global networks, including Taylor's own work, emphasizes functional connections over simple hierarchies. However, analyses using nighttime lights (NTL) often rely on aggregate radiance, conflating diverse urban processes like logistics expansion and residential sprawl. This limits our understanding of how different functional components of urban networks grow, interact, and drive regional development.
proposals.hypothesis: Persistent NTL clusters can be classified into distinct, stable socio-economic functional types (e.g., logistics hubs, administrative cores, residential corridors). The evolution of the inter-urban network is best characterized by the changing connectivity and dominance relationships between these functional typologies, rather than by aggregate luminosity alone.
proposals.method: We will first apply a multi-temporal clustering algorithm (e.g., DBSCAN) to VIIRS annual NTL composites (2012-2025) to identify persistent light clusters. Each cluster will be classified into a functional typology using a supervised Random Forest model trained on fused spatial infrastructure data (ports, highways, rail, building footprints from OpenStreetMap, land cover). Network evolution will then be modeled by constructing annual directional graphs where nodes are functional typologies and weighted edges represent significant radiance correlations or infrastructure linkages between them, tracked using temporal network analysis.
proposals.contribution: This research provides a novel methodological framework to decompose and track the functional anatomy of urban network growth. It contributes to urban studies by operationalizing the 'world city network' concept at a sub-metropolitan, functional typology level, and to remote sensing by moving NTL analysis from measurement of activity to inference of function and connection.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in fusing multi-temporal NTL with infrastructure data not to correlate, but to classify persistent clusters into functional types *before* analyzing their network dynamics, thereby disentangling the structural evolution of urban systems from mere radiance increase.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Signal in the Urban Luminous Landscape: Quantifying the Historical and Rhetorical Correlates of City Light Networks
proposals.motivation: In an era of globalization, the physical morphology of cities, as seen in night-time light patterns, is often interpreted as evidence of a convergent, borderless world. However, this interpretation often overlooks the deep historical structures, such as colonial and trade networks, that continue to shape urban development. This research seeks to rigorously test the relative influence of historical ancestry versus contemporary geopolitical alignment on urban form, providing a critical, data-driven perspective on debates about global convergence.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial and intensity configuration of city lights exhibits a significant phylogenetic signal traceable to historical connection networks (e.g., imperial or trade blocs), and the strength of this historical signal will be inversely correlated with the prevalence of 'borderless world' rhetoric in a city's media and political discourse.
proposals.method: We will construct a phylogenetic tree of cities using historical data on colonial administration, major trade routes, and lingua franca zones as proxies for common 'ancestry'. For a global sample of cities, we will extract metrics of light intensity and spatial configuration from VIIRS Day/Night Band satellite data (pre-2026). We will then apply modified phylogenetic comparative methods, specifically Pagel's lambda and Blomberg's K statistics, to quantify the signal of historical ancestry in these light metrics. Finally, we will use automated content analysis (e.g., topic modeling) of news archives and political texts from each city to score 'globalist' rhetoric and correlate this with the phylogenetic signal strength.
proposals.contribution: This work will pioneer the application of phylogenetic comparative methods in urban studies, offering a novel quantitative framework to disentangle historical path-dependency from contemporary convergence. It will produce an empirical, network-based assessment of the link between material urban form and ideological discourse, advancing Peter Taylor's longstanding research on world city networks and their connections.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct methodological analogy from evolutionary biology to urban network science and in its explicit operationalization and testing of the theoretical link between physical urban signatures and political-economic rhetoric using a phylogenetic lens.

proposals.title: The Visible Network: How Nighttime Lights Imagery Shapes Globalist Rhetoric in Policy and Media Discourses
proposals.motivation: The 'Blue Marble' and 'City Lights' satellite images have become iconic representations of a connected, borderless world, frequently invoked in discourses on globalization and climate change. However, the specific role of these visualizations in legitimizing or challenging 'One World' and borderless world narratives remains under-theorized. This study addresses a critical gap in understanding how the material artifact of the global city network, made visible through remote sensing, is rhetorically mobilized in political and public debate.
proposals.hypothesis: The widespread circulation of composite nighttime lights imagery, particularly those highlighting dense trans-regional corridors (e.g., Europe-Asia), actively reinforces 'One World' and border-eroding discourses by providing a seemingly objective, scientific visualization of planetary-scale urban connectivity. This visual rhetoric is strategically deployed more frequently by actors advocating for global governance and integrated markets.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods digital discourse analysis. First, using a corpus of policy documents, NGO reports, and news media (2010-2025), we will identify key texts employing 'One World' rhetoric. We will then use a pre-trained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) and keyword co-occurrence network analysis to detect and quantify the association between this rhetoric and references to/use of nighttime lights imagery. Second, a qualitative visual rhetoric analysis will examine how specific images (e.g., NASA's Black Marble) are cropped, captioned, and deployed in argumentative structures.
proposals.contribution: This research will bridge Peter Taylor's world city network analysis with critical geopolitics and visual culture studies. It will produce a novel framework for understanding the socio-technical construction of globalism, demonstrating how scientific data visualizations become potent political tools. The findings will contribute to debates on the perceptual foundations of globalization and the power of images in shaping policy imaginaries.
proposals.novelty: While city networks and global rhetoric are studied separately, this project innovatively links the *visualization* of the former to the production of the latter. It moves beyond mapping connectivity to analyze how those maps are consumed and rhetorically weaponized, a non-obvious step given the typical treatment of such imagery as neutral scientific fact.

proposals.title: Ecological Network Resilience in the Global Urban System: Modeling World-City Networks as Trophic and Habitat Networks
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on world-city networks, including Taylor's own work, has effectively mapped connectivity but lacks robust frameworks for analyzing systemic resilience to shocks like financial crises, pandemics, or trade disruptions. Ecological network analysis (ENA) offers mature, quantitative theories for understanding stability, redundancy, and vulnerability in complex systems, which remain underexplored in urban studies. Applying these models can provide a novel, predictive lens on how the global urban system might withstand or collapse under stress.
proposals.hypothesis: World-city networks, when modeled as ecological networks (e.g., food webs for resource flows, habitat networks for knowledge/information flows), will exhibit structural properties—such as modularity, connectance, and trophic coherence—that predict their functional resilience to simulated node or link failures. We hypothesize that networks with higher modularity and specific keystone structures will demonstrate greater robustness.
proposals.method: We will construct directed, weighted networks of world cities using pre-2025 data on air passenger traffic (resource/energy flow analog) and inter-city corporate headquarters-subsidiary linkages (trophic interaction analog). Using established ENA algorithms in R (e.g., `enaR`, `igraph`), we will calculate key metrics: network connectance, trophic level (using the Levine 1980 algorithm), modularity (using a Louvain-like method), and keystone species indices (e.g., topological importance, dynamical impact). Resilience will be tested via sequential node/link removal simulations, comparing observed network fragmentation against randomized null models.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a rigorous, transdisciplinary framework for assessing urban system resilience, moving beyond descriptive connectivity maps. It will produce a typology of city roles (e.g., keystone hubs, redundant connectors, peripheral specialists) based on ecological function. The findings will offer policymakers quantitative tools to identify critical vulnerabilities and reinforce the robustness of global urban-economic networks.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis is common in urban research, the explicit translation of food web and habitat connectivity models—with their specific metrics and dynamical assumptions—to the world-city system is novel. This approach goes beyond social network analysis by applying the well-developed causal theories of ecosystem stability directly to the urban realm, a synthesis not yet fully realized in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: The Minimum World City Network: Identifying the Skeletal Core for Global Functional Connectivity
proposals.motivation: In an era of globalization, world cities are theorized as the critical nodes for transnational flows of capital, information, and people. However, the resilience and efficiency of this network is poorly understood. Determining the smallest set of cities required to maintain a globally connected functional network addresses a core problem in urban studies and economic geography: what is the irreducible skeletal structure of globalization, and how vulnerable is it to strategic disruption?
proposals.hypothesis: The global city network possesses a small, non-random core set of cities whose removal would critically fragment global functional connectivity, and this minimum viable network is significantly smaller than the set of cities typically identified as 'Alpha' world cities.
proposals.method: The research will construct a multi-layered, directed network using pre-2026 data on advanced producer service firm offices (GaWC method), airline passenger flows, and internet backbone connections. We will apply a modified greedy algorithm for the minimum connected dominating set problem, weighted by node centrality and inter-layer edge redundancy, to identify the smallest set of cities that keeps the global network functionally connected. Sensitivity analysis will test the robustness of this core against simulated node failures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical specification of a 'minimum viable network' for globalization, moving beyond descriptive rankings. It will offer a new metric for assessing systemic risk in the world city network and contribute a novel, optimization-based framework for analyzing urban network resilience and hierarchy.
proposals.novelty: While world city research has mapped connectivity, it has not formally solved for the minimum set required for global coverage. This applies a hard computational optimization problem (the minimum connected dominating set) to the soft, relational data of urban studies, creating a novel synthesis of network science and critical geography.

proposals.title: Discontiguous and Overlapping Hinterlands: A Global Network Analysis of Urban Influence Beyond Spatial Contiguity
proposals.motivation: Urban and economic geography has long relied on models (e.g., Central Place Theory) that assume a city's hinterland is a spatially contiguous and exclusive territory. This assumption is increasingly untenable in a globalized world characterized by long-distance, specialized flows of information, capital, and people. Re-examining this foundational concept is critical for accurately modeling 21st-century urban systems, regional planning, and understanding the true spatial structure of economic influence.
proposals.hypothesis: The functional hinterlands of major world cities are not spatially contiguous or exclusive but are instead fragmented, overlapping networks defined by specific flow types (e.g., air passenger traffic, corporate ownership, project finance), creating a complex, multi-layered geography of urban influence.
proposals.method: The research will employ a multi-layer network analysis framework. Using pre-2026 global datasets (e.g., OAG air passenger flows, Orbis corporate ownership links, fDi Markets project data), we will construct separate directed weighted networks for each flow type. For each city node, we will apply a customized community detection algorithm (e.g., a flow-based adaptation of the Louvain method) to identify its functional 'hinterland' as the set of nodes most strongly connected to it within each network layer. Spatial analysis in GIS will then quantify the contiguity and overlap of these hinterland sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, empirically grounded theory of non-contiguous urban hinterlands, challenging a core geographical assumption. It will offer a new methodological toolkit for mapping functional urban regions in a global context and yield practical insights for infrastructure planning and economic development policy that account for discontinuous spatial relationships.
proposals.novelty: While Peter Taylor's prior work on world city networks maps inter-city connections, this proposal innovates by systematically inverting the analysis to define hinterlands *from* those networks, explicitly testing their spatial properties against the classical contiguity model, which remains a default in regional science.

proposals.title: Network Primacy in the World City System: A Formal Framework for Deriving Hierarchies from Connectivity
proposals.motivation: The study of world cities has long been dominated by hierarchical models, ranking cities based on attributes like corporate headquarters or advanced producer services. This perspective, while influential, risks obscuring the complex, multi-scalar, and often non-hierarchical connections—in finance, migration, knowledge, and infrastructure—that constitute the actual urban system. A shift in perspective is needed to better explain the resilience, adaptability, and polycentric development of 21st-century urbanization.
proposals.hypothesis: The global urban system is fundamentally structured as a multiplex, weighted network of flows and relations, and observed urban hierarchies are emergent properties or special cases derived from specific projections of this underlying network structure.
proposals.method: The research will construct a multiplex network dataset for a global set of cities, integrating pre-2026 data on air passenger flows, inter-firm ownership/project networks, co-publication ties, and digital infrastructure (e.g., Internet backbone). Using network analysis algorithms (e.g., community detection via the Louvain method, centrality measures, and k-core decomposition), we will first characterize the system's connective topology. Hierarchies will then be derived as secondary outputs through techniques like eigenvector centrality ranking or by applying a dominance algorithm to a directed, weighted sub-network (e.g., of corporate control), testing which network configurations produce stable hierarchies versus flat or heterarchical structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, replicable methodological framework for moving beyond hierarchy-first urban analysis. It will contribute to theoretical debates in urban studies by rigorously demonstrating how hierarchical outcomes emerge from networked processes, offering a more nuanced tool for policymakers to identify a city's functional roles beyond simple rank.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis is used in urban studies, this proposal inverts the standard analytical sequence by treating hierarchy as a derivative, not a primary, structure. It synthesizes Taylor's prior work on global connections into a unified formal model that challenges the ontological primacy of hierarchy in the world city discourse.

proposals.title: Beyond the Dichotomy: A Continuous Spectrum Model of Urbanism for Global Comparative Analysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 urban studies and policy remain heavily reliant on the categorical distinction between 'town' and 'city,' a binary often based on arbitrary administrative or population thresholds. This obscures the functional complexity of urban places, hinders nuanced comparative analysis, and fails to capture the multi-scalar nature of contemporary urbanization where even smaller settlements can exhibit 'city-like' global functions.
proposals.hypothesis: Urban places can be more accurately characterized by their relative positions on continuous, multi-dimensional spectra of 'town-ness' (localized, embedded functions) and 'city-ness' (extra-local, connective functions), with all settlements possessing a unique blend of both attributes.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-scalar dataset for a global sample of urban places, integrating variables for local embeddedness (e.g., employment diversity, service provision) and global connectivity (e.g., air passenger flows, advanced producer service firm networks, digital infrastructure). Using principal component analysis (PCA) to reduce dimensionality, we will derive orthogonal axes representing 'town-ness' and 'city-ness.' We will then apply cluster analysis (e.g., k-means) not to create firm categories, but to identify archetypal profiles along these spectra and map their global distribution.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, empirically grounded framework for comparing urban places beyond simple hierarchies. It will challenge the categorical thinking dominant in urban theory and offer policymakers a more precise tool for understanding the specific functional profiles of settlements within regional and global networks.
proposals.novelty: While global city research measures world-city connectivity, it typically excludes smaller settlements. This proposal innovates by applying the logic of network connectivity and functional analysis to the entire urban continuum, explicitly theorizing and measuring the co-presence of local and global functions as spectra rather than types.

proposals.title: The Urban Duality: A Global Network Analysis of Town-ness and City-ness Processes
proposals.motivation: Urban theory has long distinguished between the localized, embedded social relations of 'towns' and the globalized, externalized flows of 'cities,' but this remains a conceptual framework. With the rise of global city network research and new data on digital and material connections, there is an urgent need to empirically ground this duality. Testing it matters because it clarifies the co-existing social and economic logics that shape inequality, governance, and resilience within contemporary urban systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that real-world urban systems are structured by two distinct, measurable network layers: one (city-ness) characterized by long-range, specialized, corporate-service connections following a hierarchical, scale-free structure, and another (town-ness) characterized by shorter-range, multiplex, socio-cultural connections following a more regional, small-world structure.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-layer network for a global sample of 200 metropolitan regions. The 'city-ness' layer will use pre-2026 inter-city data from the Globalization and World Cities (GaWC) network (advanced producer service firms) and air passenger flows. The 'town-ness' layer will use domestic migration patterns, sister-city agreements, and co-mention networks from localized news sources. We will compare network topologies using algorithms for calculating scale-free properties (power-law fitting via maximum likelihood estimation), small-world metrics (clustering coefficient and characteristic path length), and community detection (using modularity optimization with the Louvain algorithm).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first large-scale, quantitative validation of the dual process framework, moving it from metaphor to measurable theory. It will contribute a novel methodological template for decomposing urban networks and offer empirical insights into which cities are 'global' but lack 'town' cohesion, or vice versa, with implications for urban policy.
proposals.novelty: While GaWC and others have mapped global city networks, and social physicists have studied urban scaling, no study has yet designed a parallel network architecture to explicitly measure and contrast these two theorized processes of urbanism within the same set of places.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Networked City: A Framework for Measuring Interurban Connection Flows
proposals.motivation: The 'global city' literature, pioneered by Sassen and others, has long argued that a city's power derives from its position in global networks of finance, information, and control. However, empirical measurement of these relational 'city-ness' flows remains fragmented, often relying on proxy corporate presence data. A systematic, multi-dimensional, and directly measurable set of network metrics is needed to move beyond static hierarchies and capture the dynamic, functional connections that constitute world city networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a city's 'global' or 'world' function can be robustly quantified by a composite index derived from measurable, directed flows across distinct network layers—specifically, air passenger traffic, inter-firm project collaboration, and digital information exchange.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-layer network model for a global sample of 150 cities. Layers will be built from: 1) OAG air passenger flow data (2019) using a gravity model to estimate origin-destination matrices; 2) co-location and project partnership data from the Orbis database for advanced producer service firms (e.g., law, finance, consulting); and 3) hyperlink and mention networks between city-based digital media outlets, scraped via the Common Crawl archive. For each layer, we will calculate node-level metrics (weighted degree, betweenness, eigenvector centrality) and inter-layer correlations using the MultiRank algorithm to derive a composite 'connection flow' index.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first integrated, flow-based metric suite for empirically comparing cities' network roles, moving the field from descriptive typologies to testable quantitative models. It will offer urban researchers and policymakers a standardized tool to diagnose a city's specific network strengths and vulnerabilities, grounding theoretical debates about connectivity in observable data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes single networks (e.g., GaWC's interlock model for firms), this proposal innovates by fusing physical, organizational, and digital flow data into a multi-layer framework, capturing the distinct but interconnected channels through which 'city-ness' is performed and sustained.

proposals.title: Complexity Regimes in World City Networks: A Multi-Scale Analysis of Hierarchical and Distributed Connectivity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on world cities, including Taylor's own work, has established that cities are connected through global networks of corporate, financial, and informational flows. However, these analyses often treat connectivity as a singular, aggregate property, potentially obscuring distinct underlying processes. Complexity theory offers a framework to differentiate between processes that create hierarchical, centralized structures and those that foster distributed, polycentric webs, which is crucial for understanding the resilience and evolution of the urban world-system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the global urban system exhibits at least two distinct complexity regimes: a top-down, hierarchical process driven by advanced producer service firms that reinforces a known world city hierarchy, and a bottom-up, distributed process driven by digital platform connectivity and mid-tier city alliances that creates emergent, non-hierarchical structures.
proposals.method: We will construct multi-layer networks from pre-2025 data: a corporate interlock layer (using GaWC methods from firm office locations) and a digital connectivity layer (using geolocated Twitter/FirmX mentions or inter-city internet traffic). We will apply complexity science metrics to each layer, including degree distribution analysis to test for scale-free (hierarchical) vs. exponential (distributed) topologies, and measures of modularity and betweenness centrality. The two layers will then be compared using a multi-dimensional scaling (MDS) and correlation analysis of their respective city centrality vectors to evaluate their congruence or divergence.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, empirically grounded typology of urban connectivity processes, moving beyond a single 'global city' metric. It will contribute to complexity-informed urban studies by specifying how different network formation rules produce different systemic outcomes. The findings will offer policymakers a clearer framework for strategizing city connectivity in an increasingly multi-polar world.
proposals.novelty: While complexity concepts are often invoked in urban studies, this proposal's novelty lies in its operationalization of distinct 'complexity regimes' through a comparative, multi-layer network analysis of established and emerging connectivity data, directly testing for a decoupling of traditional and digital urban hierarchies.

proposals.title: Intra-Urban Dualism: Measuring Town-ness and City-ness in the Relational Fabric of Metropolitan Neighborhoods
proposals.motivation: Peter J. Taylor's world city network research, grounded in the town-ness/city-ness dualism, has successfully analyzed global inter-city relations. However, this powerful relational framework has not been systematically applied at the intra-urban scale. Understanding how neighborhoods within a single metropolis function as either inward-looking 'towns' or outward-oriented 'city' nodes is critical for addressing pre-2026 urban challenges like socio-spatial polarization, service delivery, and the differential impacts of globalization within city borders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neighborhoods within a major global city (e.g., London, New York) can be empirically classified along a spectrum from 'town-ness' (characterized by strong, localized, internal social and economic ties) to 'city-ness' (characterized by extensive, extra-local connections integrating the neighborhood into wider metropolitan and global circuits), and that this classification reveals distinct patterns of resource access and socio-economic function.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-method, data-driven approach. First, we will construct a multi-layered network for a case-study city using pre-2026 data: social media geotags (e.g., Twitter/X, Foursquare) to map hyper-local social activity (town-ness), and business establishment directories (e.g., Dun & Bradstreet) or professional services firm branch locations to map formal economic networks (city-ness). We will then apply and adapt Taylor's Interlocking Network Model (INM) algorithm, treating neighborhoods as nodes and co-presence of social activity or advanced producer service firms as the primary interlock. Community detection algorithms (e.g., Louvain method) and centrality measures will be used to classify neighborhoods on the town-ness/city-ness spectrum.
proposals.contribution: This research will scale down a seminal theoretical framework in urban studies, providing a novel methodological toolkit for intra-urban analysis. It will contribute empirical evidence on the micro-geographies of globalization and generate practical insights for urban planners on neighborhood connectivity, potentially informing policies related to transit, economic development, and community investment.
proposals.novelty: While the town-ness/city-ness concept is established for inter-city analysis, its rigorous operationalization for neighborhood-level relations within a single city is unprecedented. The novelty lies in transposing a global-scale analytical model to the local scale, creating a new lens to understand metropolitan internal structure.

proposals.title: The Minimum Connectivity Threshold: A Network-Theoretic Definition of City-ness
proposals.motivation: The concept of the 'global city' has been extensively analyzed, but a fundamental question remains unanswered: what is the minimum network connectivity required for a place to be considered a city? Pre-2026 literature focuses on the upper echelons of connectivity (e.g., Sassen, Taylor), but lacks a formal, operationalizable lower bound. This gap hinders comparative urban studies and the analysis of emergent urban forms, particularly in the Global South and in polycentric regions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that 'city-ness' is not a binary attribute but a continuous function of a place's position within inter-urban networks, and that a quantifiable minimum threshold of multi-scalar connectivity exists, below which a settlement fails to exhibit key functional characteristics of a city.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-layer network model using pre-2026 data sources (e.g., GaWC, airline schedules, corporate headquarters). The core method involves applying a modified minimum cut/maximum flow algorithm (e.g., Edmonds-Karp) in reverse: instead of finding maximum flow, we will iteratively prune the weakest links from a global network of candidate settlements until their connectivity to the network core falls below a level that sustains key urban functions (measured via regression against established urban indicators like economic diversification). This identifies the 'minimum cut' that defines the network boundary of city-ness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal, network-based definition of a city's lower bound, moving beyond population or administrative criteria. It will offer a new metric for comparing cities of different sizes and developmental stages and generate a novel typology of settlements based on their network vulnerability and resilience.
proposals.novelty: While existing work maps strong connections between major cities, this proposal inverts the problem to define the *weakest possible* connections that still constitute a city. It applies flow algorithms not for optimization, but for ontological classification within urban studies.

proposals.title: Beyond Presence: Revenue-Weighted Firm Networks and the Reconfiguration of Global Urban Connectivity
proposals.motivation: The GaWC research network's foundational methodology for measuring world city connectivity treats all advanced producer service (APS) firm offices as equal contributors to a city's global network. This assumption, while operationally pragmatic, is at odds with economic reality, where firms vary drastically in size, revenue, and influence. A more nuanced, weighted measure is needed to better capture the actual economic power and functional specialization flowing through inter-city corporate networks, moving beyond a simple binary of presence/absence.
proposals.hypothesis: Weighting a city's connectivity by the revenue or global network size of its constituent APS firms will significantly alter the hierarchical ranking and cluster membership of world cities, revealing a connectivity structure more closely aligned with economic power and corporate control than with mere office counts.
proposals.method: The study will build upon the established GaWC interlocking network model. For a defined set of global APS firms (e.g., in finance, law, advertising), we will augment the standard binary (0/1) city-firm matrix with a weight for each firm-city pair. Weights will be derived from firm-level global revenue (normalized) and/or a measure of the firm's own global network size (e.g., number of major international offices). Connectivity between city-pairs (i) and (j) will then be calculated as the sum of the products of the weighted firm presences: C_ij = Σ (w_ir * w_jr) for all firms r, where 'w' is the firm-specific weight. The resulting weighted connectivity matrices will be analyzed using principal components analysis and hierarchical clustering, and compared to the standard unweighted model.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a more economically grounded and empirically precise metric for world city network analysis. It will challenge and refine existing city hierarchies, potentially elevating cities that host the headquarters or major revenue-generating nodes of dominant firms. The work will contribute a replicable methodological advancement to the field of urban studies and economic geography.
proposals.novelty: While the concept of weighting is intuitive, its systematic application to the GaWC model with real firm financial/network data to recalibrate global urban hierarchies represents a significant, yet-to-be-fully-realized empirical advance beyond the foundational 'interlocking network' concept established by Taylor, Derudder, and others.

proposals.title: The Network as Driver: A Causal Analysis of Inter-City Connectivity and Advanced Producer Service Firm Location
proposals.motivation: Saskia Sassen's 'global city' thesis and subsequent world city network (WCN) research, including Taylor's own interlocking network model, have established a strong correlation between the presence of major firms and a city's connectivity. However, the dominant analytical frame treats firm location as the primary determinant of network structure. This leaves a critical question unanswered: does the pre-existing network of connections between cities itself act as a causal force, attracting new firms and shaping subsequent corporate geography?
proposals.hypothesis: A city's position within the global inter-urban network, measured by its connectivity to other cities, exerts a significant causal influence on the subsequent location decisions of advanced producer service (APS) firms, independent of traditional agglomeration factors like market size or local institutions.
proposals.method: We will construct a longitudinal, two-mode (city-firm) network dataset for APS firms (e.g., finance, law, advertising) across 150+ global cities from 2000-2025. Using Taylor's established interlocking network model, we will compute annual city connectivity matrices. We will then employ a panel vector autoregression (PVAR) model with Granger causality tests to analyze the temporal precedence and mutual influence between annual connectivity scores and firm location counts. This will be complemented by a fixed-effects regression model with lagged connectivity as a key independent variable, controlling for GDP, population, and institutional quality.
proposals.contribution: This research will move the field from correlation to causation in WCN analysis. It will provide empirical evidence for a recursive, co-evolutionary model of cities and networks, where connectivity is both an outcome and a driver of urban economic development. The findings will offer a new theoretical lens for urban policymakers seeking to leverage external network positions for economic growth.
proposals.novelty: While the correlation is well-studied, explicitly testing for the causal direction from network connectivity to firm location inverts the standard analytical assumption. Applying time-series econometric techniques (PVAR/Granger causality) to longitudinal WCN data represents a significant methodological advance over cross-sectional or descriptive network analyses prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: Intra-Metropolitan Polycentricity: Modeling Advanced Producer Service Networks Across Districts of London and Tokyo
proposals.motivation: While the Globalization and World Cities (GaWC) research network has successfully mapped the global urban hierarchy, the internal functional structure of world cities themselves remains a 'black box'. Understanding how connectivity is organized *within* megacities is critical for planning infrastructure, managing spatial inequality, and theorizing the sub-nodal geography of globalization. This gap persists despite the recognized polycentricity of major metropolitan regions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intra-metropolitan districts function as a hierarchical network of specialized service platforms, and that the connectivity between them, measured via inter-firm project linkages, will reveal a distinct polycentric structure that does not simply mirror administrative boundaries or property values.
proposals.method: The project will apply the established GaWC methodology at a finer spatial scale. For two archetypal global cities (London, Tokyo), we will identify the headquarters and major branch offices of the same 100 leading Advanced Producer Service (APS) firms used in GaWC's intercity studies across defined intra-urban districts (e.g., London's Central Activities Zone sectors, Tokyo's special wards). Connectivity will be measured using the interlocking network model, where the co-location of firms from different sectors in two districts generates a potential for project-based interaction. Network analysis (e.g., degree centrality, community detection algorithms like the Louvain method) will then map the hierarchy and clusters of district connectivity.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce the first systematic, firm-based mapping of intra-metropolitan connectivity for world cities, offering a quantitative complement to qualitative studies of urban polycentricity. It will contribute a novel methodological framework for scaling world city analysis downward and provide empirical evidence for planners on the functional integration (or segregation) of key economic districts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate scalar transposition of a mature model for inter-urban analysis to the intra-urban realm, treating city districts as 'world cities in miniature' within a global network. This moves beyond descriptive land-use studies to model the actual organizational networks that constitute a city's internal global economic fabric.

ideas.title: Mapping the Historical Emergence and Overlay of Functional Networks on State-Homogenized Space
ideas.description: This research employs Historical GIS to trace the centuries-long process of internal state territorial standardization (e.g., cadastral surveys, administrative grids, legal homogenization). It then uses this reconstructed historical spatial substrate as a basemap to analyze the subsequent emergence and spatial logic of transnational functional networks (supply chains, epistemic communities, digital infrastructure). The core analytical innovation is to treat state homogenization not as the end goal of spatial history, but as a preparatory phase that unintentionally enabled or provoked the rise of alternative, post-territorial network geographies. This allows for a diachronic study of how the world-system's fundamental spatial units have shifted from homogenized territories to layered, competing functional networks.

ideas.title: Identifying Functional Process-Nodes: A Multi-Layer Flow Analysis to Move Beyond Geographic Containers in Urban Networks
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that any pre-defined geographic unit (district, city, region) is the primary node in global networks. Instead, it posits that the fundamental units are dynamic functional processes identifiable through patterns in multi-scalar flows. The study employs a multi-layer network analysis on diverse, high-resolution flow data to inductively map stable functional clusters. It then rigorously tests whether these process-derived nodes or traditional geographic units better explain key socio-economic outcomes, moving the debate from 'which container' to 'which function' structures the network.

ideas.title: The State as a Competitive Platform for Network Capture and Regulation
ideas.description: This model reframes the world-system by theorizing the state not as a fixed territorial container but as an adaptive institutional platform. Its primary function is to compete for jurisdictional control and revenue extraction from transnational functional networks that operate through its space. The research system would develop metrics for 'network capture' (e.g., data localization laws, tariff nodes, regulatory standards enforcement) and model state strategies as responses to network topology and flow volumes. This explains territoriality as an outcome of state-network interaction, not a pre-existing unit.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Modeling to Compare City-Network and State-Centric Spatial Logics
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodological solution from ecology and biogeography of testing competing spatial hypotheses through predictive modeling. The analogous problem involves determining whether a species' distribution is better explained by environmental suitability (an intrinsic, a-spatial logic) or by dispersal barriers and histories (a politically/physically bounded logic), solved using Species Distribution Models (SDMs). Here, we adapt this to compare the spatial logic of city networks versus traditional political boundaries in shaping global flows. We will treat specific global flows (e.g., air passengers, venture capital) as the phenomenon to be modeled. We will construct two distinct predictive frameworks: a 'city-network model' using variables like inter-city corporate linkages, airline route centrality, and digital infrastructure, and a 'state-centric model' using variables like shared membership in political unions, bilateral treaties, and contiguous borders. By training these models on historical flow data and testing their predictive accuracy against held-out or contemporary data, we can empirically determine which spatial logic—the networked or the bounded—provides a more powerful explanation for the actual geography of global integration.

ideas.title: A Multi-Layer Network Model for World-Cities Using Ecological Niche Theory
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a single global city network by adapting a solution from Community Ecology: the multi-dimensional niche model. In ecology, a species' niche is defined by its position along multiple independent environmental gradients, allowing for complex coexistence patterns. Analogously, we propose to model a city's 'network niche' within a multi-layer relational space defined by distinct types of flow (financial, knowledge, cultural, logistical). Each flow type constitutes a separate network layer. The adapted research idea uses this framework to move beyond a single hierarchy, mapping how cities hold core/peripheral positions that vary by layer. This allows for the analysis of niche overlap (competition for similar flows) and niche complementarity (symbiotic relationships), providing a more nuanced understanding of overlapping and competing world-city networks based on differentiated flows.

ideas.title: Mapping the Channels and Constraints of Sub-State Global Engagement: A Relational Governance Analysis
ideas.description: This research systematically analyzes the specific governance channels—such as paradiplomacy, special economic zones, and transborder networks—through which sub-state regions participate in the global economy. It employs a comparative framework to measure the formal and informal autonomy of regions, assessing how national legal frameworks, fiscal policies, and political strategies simultaneously enable and constrain these activities. The outcome is a typology of regional-global engagement that moves beyond a simple bypass model to explain the complex, negotiated interdependence between sub-state, state, and supranational actors in shaping contemporary economic geography.

ideas.title: Household-Scale Network Analysis of Sub-State Global Flows
ideas.description: This research direction applies network analysis not to nation-states or corporations, but to households conceptualized as nodal territorial units. It maps the transnational flows of capital, goods, data, and social remittances that are directly orchestrated by households, bypassing traditional state-centric economic accounts. By scaling network theory down to the domestic unit, it creates a micro-founded topology of globalization, showing how household sovereignty—in managing its borders, external dependencies, and internal economies—actively weaves the larger, often informal, structures of the world-system.

ideas.title: A Multi-Layer Digital Trace Integration System for Inferring Global City Network Connectivity
ideas.description: This research system moves beyond the single-proxy approach by integrating nighttime light data with direct evidence of inter-city flows to construct a robust, multi-layer world-city network. It uses processed VIIRS/DMSP-OLS annual composites to establish baseline city 'mass' or economic activity, which serves as a prior in a Bayesian network inference model. This prior is then updated and constrained using observed connection data from air passenger schedules, internet infrastructure maps, and business project co-location databases to model layers of physical, digital, and corporate connectivity. The final output is a probabilistically validated, multi-dimensional network map that quantifies both the structure and the intensity of different types of connectivity between global cities, significantly reducing the inferential leap required from luminosity alone.

ideas.title: Network Analysis of Pre-Modern Non-State Metageographies
ideas.description: This research project applies formal network analysis and graph theory to reconstruct and analyze the flows of capital, commodities, people, and ideas within documented pre-modern and early modern metageographies that operated independently of or parallel to state borders. By constructing computational models of systems such as the Silk Road's relay trade, the Indian Ocean monsoon marketplace, or Polynesian kinship-voyaging networks, it creates a quantitative dataset of non-state spatial organization. This allows for direct structural comparison with modern global networks that bypass state control, testing whether contemporary 'novel' non-state systems are genuinely new or reactivate ancient topological patterns of interconnection.

ideas.title: Functional Urban Network Evolution: Disentangling and Tracking Typologies of Nighttime Light Clusters
ideas.description: This research moves beyond tracking aggregate radiance to model the evolution of functional urban networks. It first classifies persistent nighttime light clusters into distinct socio-economic types by fusing multi-temporal satellite imagery with spatial infrastructure data. The core analysis then tracks the growth, decline, and changing connectivity between these functionally defined typologies—such as linking the expansion of logistic hubs to port radiance or residential sprawl to highway corridors. This approach reveals the structural and functional dynamics of urban network growth, distinguishing meaningful development from mere increases in luminosity.

ideas.title: The Dark Container: How Invisible Financial Networks Drive the Resurgence of Territorial Control
ideas.description: This research investigates the symbiotic relationship between globally significant but opaque financial networks and the contemporary resurgence of strong state borders. It posits that the 'dark' nodes of capital movement—such as shadow banking hubs, special economic zones with lax oversight, and cryptocurrency infrastructures—do not merely exist in a stateless void. Instead, they create powerful incentives for states to reseal their territorial containers to capture, regulate, or weaponize these flows. The novel direction examines how states strategically reassert border control and internal surveillance not in spite of globalization, but precisely to govern the invisible, high-value networks that now constitute the core of geopolitical and economic competition, thereby inverting the assumption that visibility equates to importance in the analysis of state power.

ideas.title: Historical GIS of Non-Territorial Corporate Sovereignty: Visualizing the Counter-Homogenization of Capitalist Space
ideas.description: This research project employs Historical GIS to map and analyze the spatial strategies of non-state corporate entities (e.g., chartered companies, special economic zone operators, digital platform jurisdictions) over the same centuries-long timeframe used to study state-driven territorial standardization. By visualizing corporate legal enclaves, supply chains, and resource claims as dynamic, overlapping layers atop the homogenizing state map, it models a potential capitalist world-system organized through corporate sovereignty. The core analysis identifies whether corporate spatial practices historically aligned with, resisted, or operated independently of state territorial logics, providing an empirical basis for theorizing a post-territorial capitalist geography.

ideas.title: Applying Phylogenetic Signal Analysis to City Light Networks and Globalist Rhetoric
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from evolutionary biology to study the link between satellite imagery of city lights and global rhetoric. In phylogenetics, methods exist to disentangle whether a biological trait is inherited from common ancestry or independently evolved. Analogously, we construct a network-phylogeny of cities based on historical connections (e.g., colonial empires, major trade routes). Using satellite-derived data on city light intensity and spatial configuration, we apply modified phylogenetic signal statistics to see if light patterns are better explained by this historical 'ancestry' or by contemporary geopolitical/economic groupings. The core analysis then investigates how the strength of this signal—indicating either deep-structural continuity or convergent, borderless development—correlates with the prevalence of 'One World' and borderless world discourses in the political and media outputs of those urban centers, offering a quantitative link between visible network topology and rhetorical stance.

ideas.title: Inferring the Shadow Topology: A Multi-Method Network Model of Formal and Informal World-System Flows
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid network analysis framework to map the complete structure of transnational capital, commodity, and people flows, including both traceable and intentionally obscured circuits. It combines graph theory applied to traditional economic and trade data with probabilistic modeling techniques that use proxy indicators, digital traces, and ground-level data to infer the likely structure and volume of informal and illicit flows. The resulting multi-layered network model reveals the critical interdependencies and systemic vulnerabilities created by these shadow networks, providing a more robust basis for understanding how power and capital truly circulate outside of formal state channels.

ideas.title: Adapting Metabolic Network Analysis to Quantify Economic Connectivity from Nighttime Lights
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from Systems Biology, specifically Metabolic Network Analysis. In that field, the structure and intensity of a cell's metabolic network are inferred by measuring the flows of key metabolites between compartments. Adapting this solution, the research will treat satellite-derived nighttime light data not merely as a static brightness map, but as a dynamic proxy for the flow of economic activity and capital—the 'metabolites' of the world-city network. By analyzing the correlations, time-lags, and intensity gradients in light emissions (e.g., diurnal/weekly patterns, growth rates) between urban areas, the method will empirically infer the directionality, strength, and functional hierarchy of economic connectivity. This allows for the quantification of a live, dynamic network structure, identifying hub cities, strong dyadic links, and the relative integration of regions based on the observed 'flux' of luminous economic signal.

ideas.title: Mapping the Luminous Sovereignty of Global Networks
ideas.description: This research uses high-resolution nighttime light (NTL) data to empirically map the physical infrastructure and operational footprints of entities exercising non-territorial forms of sovereignty, such as global tech platforms, special economic zones, or transnational diaspora networks. By analyzing the intensity, stability, and connectivity patterns of light emissions, it quantifies the 'luminous sovereignty' of these actors—where their functional control (via data, law, or economic activity) manifests in and reorganizes physical space. This approach moves beyond theoretical claims to provide a spatially explicit, data-driven framework for comparing the material power of territorial states with the diffuse, network-based power of non-territorial sovereigns.

ideas.description: This research applies astrophysical methods for detecting point sources and clustering luminosity to map the 'light' emitted by multinational corporations (MNCs) and NGOs—using data like satellite-observed nighttime lights, digital transaction volumes, or social media activity as proxies for economic and social output. These luminosity maps are then used as input data for ecological niche models, framing the functional and geographic spaces vacated or under-served by states as an 'environment' in which non-state actors compete. The synergy enables a predictive, systems-level analysis of how non-state actor networks colonize, brighten, dim, or are displaced in this landscape, revealing the dynamic astrophysical 'behavior' of socio-economic ecosystems.

ideas.title: Causal Multi-Layer Urban Network Analysis: Embedding Ecological Analogues within Socio-Economic Drivers
ideas.description: This research moves beyond direct analogy by creating a causal, multi-layer network framework for urban systems. It explicitly models key socio-economic and institutional drivers (e.g., investment networks, policy coordination) as separate network layers, which are quantitatively coupled to a layer structured using ecological network principles (like mutualism or competition). The system's resilience is then tested through simulated disruptions that propagate across all layers, identifying whether failures originate in the structural 'ecology' or the enabling socio-economic substrate. This produces actionable insights into which driver layers must be fortified to enhance the resilience observed in the ecological analog.

ideas.title: Quantifying Jurisdictional Erosion: Modeling Cross-Border Flow Networks and Systemic Crisis in the World-System
ideas.description: This research program operationalizes territoriality by modeling how measurable erosions of state jurisdictional control—through illicit financial flows, corporate tax avoidance, and strategic resource diversion—destabilize the global economic system. It constructs a multilayer network model using financial, trade, and regulatory data (pre-2026) to map the channels and volumes of these 'leaks.' By integrating this structural analysis with agent-based simulations of state and corporate behavior, the project tests how specific thresholds of jurisdictional erosion precipitate cascading failures in accumulation cycles, providing empirically grounded scenarios for future systemic crisis.
