ideas.title: Planetary Containerization: Analyzing the Historical Process of Global Socio-Ecological Homogenization
ideas.description: This research project applies the historical lens of state-driven internal homogenization—the standardization of laws, economies, and identities within national borders—to the planetary scale. It investigates how analogous processes of 'planetary containerization' are being driven by global institutions, capital flows, and crisis responses, aiming to standardize management of the biosphere, climate, and human systems into a governable whole. The analysis reveals the contested politics behind efforts to create a homogenized global operating space, exposing tensions between uniformity for governance and the resilience found in socio-ecological diversity. Thus, it reframes global crises as stages in an ongoing historical project to make the Earth legible and manageable as a single, integrated container.

ideas.title: Planetary Container Leakage: Modeling the Biosphere as a Failing Territorial Unit in World-System Crisis
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the 'Earth as a single container' concept with theories of territoriality and systemic crisis to analyze the planetary biosphere as the ultimate, but increasingly leaky, territorial unit for capital. It models how the failure of this container—through climate change, transboundary pollution, and resource depletion—fundamentally degrades the material and spatial foundations of the world-system. The approach examines how these biophysical leaks precipitate and accelerate socio-economic crises by disrupting accumulation, forcing geopolitical realignments, and challenging the very logic of territorially bounded governance and economics.

ideas.title: Apply ecological food web network analysis to world-system bypass flows
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to a parallel problem in ecology—mapping complex energy and nutrient flows in food webs—to the social science context of world-system analysis. In ecology, network analysis is used to understand how energy flows through ecosystems, identifying critical species and alternative pathways that bypass traditional hierarchies. The adapted idea applies this framework to map global flows of capital, commodities, and people that circumvent state structures. By treating non-state entities as analogous to species and their interactions as trophic links, we can use ecological network metrics (e.g., centrality, modularity, robustness) to identify key hubs in bypass networks, measure the resilience of these alternative systems, and model how flows reorganize when state channels are disrupted.

ideas.title: The Conditions of Territorial Integration: A Comparative Framework for State Standardization Success and Failure
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a catalog of failures to develop a general theory of when and why state-led territorial homogenization succeeds, fails, or is negotiated. It constructs a comparative framework analyzing matched pairs of cases across different state capacities, institutional legacies, and societal contexts. The method identifies the critical junctures, resource dependencies, and legitimizing narratives that determine outcomes, distinguishing between mere resistance and effective, enduring rejection of state projects. This allows for predictive insights into the fragility and durability of administrative and cultural integration.

ideas.title: Study the inverse: crisis responses in core economies
ideas.description: Invert the focus from semi-peripheral states to core capitalist states like the US or Germany.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Materialist Framework for Political Geography

ideas.title: Explore cultural governance beyond global trade
ideas.description: Apply the performative critique to other areas of global cultural governance like environment or finance.
ideas.sourceTitle: Globalization in Question. The International Economy and the Possibilities of Governance

ideas.title: Modeling the Co-evolutionary Dynamics of State and Non-State Actors in Governance Landscapes
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic, multi-agent framework to analyze how Multinational Corporations (MNCs) and Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs) interact with and reshape state governance capacities, rather than merely occupying vacated niches. It employs longitudinal network analysis of cross-sector partnerships, geospatial modeling of service provision and capital flows, and metrics of contested legitimacy to capture bidirectional influence and adaptation. The system simulates scenarios of co-evolution, identifying conditions under which non-state actor presence erodes, supplements, or transforms state functions, moving beyond simplistic vacuum-filling models to explain complex, emergent governance ecosystems.

ideas.title: Question the novelty of the non-state-centric view
ideas.description: Explore pre-modern or historical metageographies that also organized space without state borders.
ideas.sourceTitle: World-City Network: A New Metageography?

ideas.title: Quantifying Sovereignty: A Network Topology Approach to Non-Territorial Power
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from metapopulation biology in ecology to the study of non-territorial sovereignty. In ecology, the survival of a species in a fragmented landscape is analyzed through network topology—modeling discrete habitat patches connected by migration corridors, with viability determined by connectivity, patch quality, and network resilience, not the contiguity of any single patch. This analogy is adapted by conceptualizing non-territorial sovereign entities (based on data, law, or identity) as a network of functional 'patches' (e.g., server farms, court jurisdictions, community centers). The research will develop metrics and models to quantify such sovereignty by analyzing the network's topology: the bandwidth and security of data corridors, the enforceability of legal links, the cultural flow between identity nodes, and the overall system's robustness against the removal or attack on individual nodes. This shifts the analytical framework from a binary, territory-bound concept to a scalable, functional one based on relational power and network integrity.

ideas.title: Historical GIS of Territorial Negotiation: Mapping State Standardization Against Persistent Heterogeneity
ideas.description: This research employs a multi-layered historical GIS framework to analyze the long-term dialectic between state-driven territorial homogenization and persistent socio-spatial heterogeneity. It systematically maps not only official administrative reforms, infrastructure, and cadastral systems but also layers representing customary land use, ethnic or linguistic enclaves, informal trade networks, and episodes of local resistance or adaptation. By quantifying the friction, incompleteness, and durability of alternative spatial orders, the approach models territoriality as a contested process rather than a state-monopolized outcome, revealing where and why standardization failed or was fundamentally transformed.

ideas.title: Applying Phylogenetic Methods to Map Historical Metageographies
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that a non-state-centric view of spatial organization is a novel, modern insight. By drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology, where phylogenetic methods replaced static, category-based taxonomy with dynamic lineage mapping, the project proposes a methodological adaptation. In evolutionary biology, cladistics solved the problem of obscured relationships by focusing on shared derived characteristics to build evolutionary trees. Adapted to historical metageography, the research would identify core, replicable 'spatial practices' (analogous to traits) that organize space without reference to state borders—such as nomadic range circuits, sacred geographies, or maritime trading networks—across pre-modern and historical contexts. By mapping the distribution and evolution of these practices, the study aims to construct phylogenetic-like networks of non-state spatial logic. This would demonstrate that contemporary non-state-centric views are not novel discoveries but rediscoveries or late branches of much older, diverse, and interconnected lineages of spatial thought and organization, thereby fundamentally questioning the temporality and uniqueness of the assumed modern breakthrough.

ideas.title: Assume territoriality is increasing, not declining
ideas.description: Argue new technologies and conflicts are producing more, not fewer, borders.
ideas.sourceTitle: The state as container: territoriality in the modern world-system

ideas.title: Scale up firm count for denser network
ideas.description: Increase the number of firms from 100 to several thousand to achieve a finer-grained resolution of arenas.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hierarchical Tendencies and Regional Patterns in the World City Network: A Global Urban Analysis of 234 Cities

ideas.title: Apply discursive analysis to moral geography narratives
ideas.description: Use critical discourse analysis to examine the language and texts that construct moral geographies.
ideas.sourceTitle: Boundaries and consciousness : The changing geographies of the Finnish-Russian border

ideas.title: Satellite Sovereignty: How Nighttime Imagery Recasts Historical Border Porosity as a Modern Political Tool
ideas.description: This research investigates the paradoxical role of satellite imagery of city lights in global discourse. It argues that while these images are used rhetorically to support 'One World' and borderless ideals, they simultaneously create a powerful visual model of sovereignty as a contiguous, luminous bloc—a model that is anachronistic and misleading. By fusing this with the historical understanding of borders as inherently porous and incompletely controlled, the project exposes how the seductive, top-down 'nightlights' view actively erases the complex, ground-level realities of cross-border flows, informal networks, and zones of mixed authority that have always existed. Thus, the research posits that this dominant visual rhetoric does not document a new global reality but constructs a potent ideological tool that legitimizes state power by rendering historical normativity invisible.

ideas.title: Mapping the Luminous Fractures: Nighttime Light Evolution as an Index of Failed Territorial Homogenization
ideas.description: This research uses the historical evolution of nighttime city lights not to map network growth, but to detect and analyze anomalies where the expected 'brightening' pattern of state-led territorial homogenization fails to materialize or is actively reversed. By correlating persistent dark zones, irregular light geometries, or divergent growth patterns within state borders with historical and political data, it identifies and compares cases of successful resistance to internal standardization. The approach quantitatively challenges narratives of inevitable state spatial control by revealing a 'metageography of luminous fractures,' where infrastructure, settlement, and economic activity follow alternative, non-state logics visible from space over decades.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession Models to State Internal Homogenization
ideas.description: This research adapts the model of ecological succession to trace how states standardize internal social and economic spaces over time. The analogous problem in ecology—how a biological community progresses from initial colonization to a stable climax ecosystem—provides a framework. The adapted solution involves analyzing state homogenization as a succession process: identifying distinct historical stages of standardization, the mechanisms (e.g., legal codification, infrastructure projects, cultural campaigns) that facilitate transitions between these stages, and modeling the conditions under which the homogenized 'climax state' is achieved and maintained. This approach moves beyond linear narrative to a dynamic systems analysis of state-building.

ideas.title: Examine sectoral, not national, crisis management
ideas.description: Apply the framework to how specific sectors (e.g., auto, finance) manage crisis within a state.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Materialist Framework for Political Geography

ideas.title: Scale down to roster for emerging or niche sports
ideas.description: Identify optimal cities for launching leagues in new or growing sports.
ideas.sourceTitle: A roster of world cities

ideas.title: Update roster with new franchise expansions and relocations
ideas.description: Track and incorporate new team entries and city changes since the original 1999 publication.
ideas.sourceTitle: A roster of world cities

ideas.title: Scale up to supranational unions
ideas.description: Study how mobility and trade within a union like the EU affect information aggregation in European Parliament elections.
ideas.sourceTitle: Geography of Elections

ideas.title: Epidemiological diffusion models for economic shocks
ideas.description: Applying SIR-type contagion models from epidemiology to simulate how financial crises propagate through the city network.
ideas.sourceTitle: World City Network: A global urban analysis

ideas.title: Comparative Morphometrics of Territorial Systems
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from evolutionary morphometrics to the comparative study of world-systems. Just as biologists objectively compare anatomical forms across species using landmark-based geometric morphometrics, this project will develop a method to quantitatively compare the 'shapes' of different territorial logics. By defining a common set of structural landmarks (e.g., loci of sovereignty, permeability of borders, spatial distribution of key functions) for systems like the modern nation-state, medieval empires, or indigenous kin-based territories, we can plot them in a conceptual morphospace. This analytical adaptation allows for a rigorous, empirical contrast between the modern state-container and pre-modern or non-capitalist forms, moving beyond metaphor to identify fundamental dimensions of variation and convergence in territorial organization across history and mode of production.

ideas.title: Use agent-based modeling for tourist flow simulation
ideas.description: Simulate tourist behavior and package choice dynamics in a city network using agent-based models from complexity science.
ideas.sourceTitle: Measurement of the World City Network

ideas.title: The primary problem is theoretical, not practical
ideas.description: Assume the core problem is implementation, not theory, and focus on political barriers to change.
ideas.sourceTitle: World cities and territorial states under conditions of contemporary globalization

ideas.title: Use agent-based modeling for dual processes
ideas.description: Simulate the emergence of hierarchy and network relations from agent interactions.
ideas.sourceTitle: External Urban Relational Process: Introducing Central Flow Theory to Complement Central Place Theory

ideas.title: Endogenize the number of electoral districts
ideas.description: Model how the optimal number of districts might change with trade costs and voter mobility.
ideas.sourceTitle: Geography of Elections

ideas.title: Employ multi-criteria decision analysis for city selection
ideas.description: Formalize city evaluation using weighted criteria like market size and stadium feasibility.
ideas.sourceTitle: A roster of world cities

ideas.title: Explore interplay of legal fact and social reality
ideas.description: Empirically detail the specific mechanisms of interplay between border law and borderland social complexity.
ideas.sourceTitle: Boundaries and consciousness : The changing geographies of the Finnish-Russian border

ideas.title: Scale down: study the household as container
ideas.description: Analyze the home as a mini-territorial unit with borders, sovereignty, and economic flows.
ideas.sourceTitle: The state as container: territoriality in the modern world-system

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Network Analysis to Map Urban Activity from Light Patterns
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from geophysics, specifically seismic network analysis, to the original context of using astrophysics methods on urban light patterns. In seismology, arrays of sensors use algorithms like beamforming and matched-field processing to locate seismic events, filter noise, and characterize subsurface structures from wave arrivals. The adapted research idea applies these techniques to time-series satellite data of city light emissions. By treating localized increases in luminosity as 'urban seismic events,' we can use seismic-style array processing to pinpoint their origin, separate overlapping light sources from different urban functions, and trace the spatiotemporal propagation of activity through the city's network. This allows for the dynamic mapping of functional connectivity and rhythmic flows within urban systems, based on their 'seismic' light signatures.

ideas.title: Symmetric interlocking versus hierarchical command flows
ideas.description: The interlocking network model assumes symmetric flows, but headquarters-subsidiary relationships create fundamentally asymmetric power structures.
ideas.sourceTitle: World City Network: A global urban analysis

ideas.title: Polycentric Earth: Modeling Nested Socio-Ecological Systems for Legitimate Global Governance
ideas.description: This research rejects the 'single container' metaphor in favor of a polycentric model of Earth's biosphere as a complex of nested, interdependent socio-ecological systems. It develops an analytical framework and computational models to map material and energy flows alongside political and economic power structures across local, regional, and global scales. The core objective is to design and simulate governance mechanisms that can effectively address planetary crises like climate change and biodiversity loss through coordinated but context-sensitive interventions, ensuring legitimacy, equity, and robust implementation across diverse political realities.

ideas.title: Social network analysis of question exchanges
ideas.description: Use SNA to map knowledge flow and roles within the community of designers.
ideas.sourceTitle: World cities in a world-system: Contents

ideas.title: Apply conceptual genealogy from philosophy
ideas.description: Trace the historical formation of key concepts like 'global individual' in democratic theory.
ideas.sourceTitle: Globalization in Question. The International Economy and the Possibilities of Governance

ideas.title: Scale down to neighborhood-level governance
ideas.description: Apply the subsidiarity vs. federalism debate to conflicts between municipalities and neighborhood associations.
ideas.sourceTitle: World cities and territorial states under conditions of contemporary globalization

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Compartment Models to Diagnose Systemic Risk in City Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core methodological solution from epidemiology—compartmental modeling of disease spread—to the context of urban network resilience. In epidemiology, models like SIR simulate how a pathogen moves through a contact network, defining states and transition rates to predict outbreaks and test interventions. The adapted research applies this framework to world-city networks, modeling cities as nodes in states of susceptibility, exposure, infection, or recovery from a systemic shock. By using data on inter-city connections to parameterize transmission and recovery rates, the model can diagnose network vulnerabilities, simulate cascade scenarios, and evaluate the effectiveness of targeted policy interventions designed to increase systemic resilience, analogous to vaccination or quarantine in a public health context.

ideas.title: Scale up: UK voluntary CR model in other countries
ideas.description: Test the transferability of the UK's voluntary, sector-specific CR approach to different national contexts.
ideas.sourceTitle: Strategy and Financial Policy in UK Small Firms

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Theory to Map Platform Territorialization
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from ecology. The analogous problem is understanding how biological entities partition and control territory within an ecosystem, which is solved using the framework of the 'ecological niche'—a model focusing on functional position, resource use, and competitive interactions. Adapting this solution, the research will map how cyber domains and platforms replicate state-like territorial logics. It will treat digital platforms as analogous to species, analyzing their 'virtual niches' defined by unique combinations of governance structures, algorithmic affordances, data economies, and user behavioral dependencies. This approach allows for a systematic, relational mapping of how platform territories are established, compete, and dominate, moving beyond physical geography to reveal the functional logics of control in virtual spaces.

ideas.title: The Historical Metageography of Resealing: A Typology of Forces that Reassemble Territorial Containers
ideas.description: This research investigates the forces that cause a resurgence of strong territorial borders by situating them within a long historical cycle of competing metageographies. It posits that the modern 'container' state is not the default but a historically contingent form, and that resealing events occur when specific material, ideological, or ecological conditions make non-state-centric spatial organization (e.g., networked, imperial, or kinship-based) untenable or undesirable. By comparing contemporary drivers of rebordering—such as pandemics, climate migration, or digital sovereignty—with historical precedents where fluid spaces crystallized into hard borders (or vice versa), the project aims to develop a typology of 'resealing triggers.' This framework moves beyond the state/non-state binary to explain how and why different spatial orders succeed one another.

ideas.title: Applying Immunological 'Barrier' Theory to Geopolitical Territoriality
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that globalization and technology lead to a decline in territoriality. Using an analogy from immunology, it posits that the problem resembles the evolution of the 'mucosal firewall' concept, where increased exposure to pathogens (analogous to global flows of people, data, and capital) does not eliminate biological barriers but makes them more numerous, sophisticated, and dynamically regulated. The analogous solution was to stop viewing barriers as simple, static lines and instead measure their functional, adaptive, and multi-layered nature. Adapted to geopolitics, this means researching territoriality not as the presence or absence of a border fence, but as a system of evolving 'geopolitical firewalls.' The adapted idea investigates how new technologies and conflicts produce a proliferation of borders—cyber sovereignties, biometric checkpoints, exclusive economic zones, and legally enforced data borders—that are often invisible but functionally robust. The research would map the density and adaptive logic of these layered controls, testing the hypothesis that perceived threats trigger a systemic, immune-like response that increases the overall territorial 'surface area' and defensive activity of states, even as traditional wall-building continues.

ideas.title: Empirical study of non-state territorial control
ideas.description: Analyze how MNCs, NGOs, and diasporas exert territorial control beyond state sovereignty.
ideas.sourceTitle: The state as container: territoriality in the modern world-system

ideas.title: Apply ecological niche modeling to non-state actors
ideas.description: Model how MNCs and NGOs occupy spatial-functional niches vacated by states.
ideas.sourceTitle: The state as container: territoriality in the modern world-system

ideas.title: Use community detection algorithms from physics
ideas.description: Apply modularity optimization to identify evolving city clusters beyond geographic regions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Specification of the World City Network

ideas.title: Relax the state-centric view of the border
ideas.description: Analyze borders where the legal/political fact is contested or defined by non-state actors.
ideas.sourceTitle: Boundaries and consciousness : The changing geographies of the Finnish-Russian border

ideas.title: Dynamic model with sequential moves
ideas.description: Model economic agglomeration and political sorting as a sequential process rather than simultaneous.
ideas.sourceTitle: Geography of Elections

ideas.title: Corporate Sovereignty as a Modular Protocol: Adapting Blockchain's Consensus Mechanisms
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by using an analogy from distributed systems. The parallel problem is achieving order and sovereignty without a central, territorial authority, which in blockchain is solved through decentralized consensus protocols. Adapting this solution, the research would theorize a capitalist world-economy where corporate sovereignty is decoupled from territorial states and instead organized through non-territorial, protocol-based governance. Firms would exist as decentralized autonomous organizations (DAOs) or similar entities, their 'sovereign' power emerging from the cryptographic and algorithmic enforcement of their corporate charter on a distributed ledger. This transforms capitalism's foundational need for a territorial state into a need for robust, self-executing code and market-based competition among governance protocols, fundamentally restructuring the political economy of global capital.

ideas.title: Urban Radiative Transfer: A Physics-Based Framework for Decomposing and Modeling City Light Networks
ideas.description: This research develops a novel 'Urban Radiative Transfer' framework to rigorously analyze city light patterns. Instead of directly applying point-source astronomy algorithms, it first physically decomposes the urban light field into its emission and reflection components using hyperspectral data and material databases. It then adapts radiative transfer equations from astrophysics to model how light propagates and scatters within the complex urban geometry, isolating the signatures of human activity from the structural background. This physics-based approach enables the robust, quantitative mapping of dynamic city networks, such as traffic flow and economic activity hubs, from remote sensing data.

ideas.title: Polycentric Corporate Sovereignties: A Meta-Institutional Framework for Non-Territorial Governance Production
ideas.description: This research theorizes a capitalist world-economy where sovereign corporate entities operate without fixed territory. Its core innovation is a framework of competing, non-territorial meta-institutions—specialized providers of legal systems, security, dispute resolution, and regulatory standards. Corporate sovereignties voluntarily subscribe to these meta-institutions via binding contracts, creating a dynamic market for governance. This system directly addresses the public goods problem by institutionalizing their production and enforcement, while the competitive pressure between meta-institutions replaces the static authority of the territorial state.

ideas.title: Question the global parameters as a stable construct
ideas.description: Treat global neoliberal parameters as highly unstable and contested, not just reconstituted.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Materialist Framework for Political Geography

ideas.title: Scale up: analyze Earth as a single container
ideas.description: Treat the planetary biosphere as the ultimate territorial unit facing global crises.
ideas.sourceTitle: The state as container: territoriality in the modern world-system

ideas.title: Modeling Non-State Actor Niche Dynamics in Leaking World-System Containers
ideas.description: This research fuses ecological niche modeling of non-state actors (MNCs, NGOs) with world-systems analysis of 'leaking' territorial containers to create a novel framework. It investigates how these actors colonize spatial-functional niches vacated by weakening states, not merely as passive occupants but as active agents that alter the permeability and integrity of the territorial 'container' itself. By modeling this reciprocal relationship, the research can predict how specific configurations of non-state niche occupation accelerate or decelerate systemic cycles of accumulation, thereby identifying likely epicenters and pathways for future world-system crises. This moves beyond static descriptions of actor presence or abstract crisis cycles to a dynamic, agent-influenced model of systemic fragility.

ideas.title: Compare fuzzy classification with other network methods
ideas.description: Validate the fuzzy arena results by comparing them with clusters from other network community detection algorithms.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hierarchical Tendencies and Regional Patterns in the World City Network: A Global Urban Analysis of 234 Cities

ideas.title: Phase Transitions in Social Systems: Modeling the Resealing of State Borders
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from statistical physics to model the resurgence of strong borders and state territorial control. The analogous problem is understanding phase transitions, such as freezing, where a system rapidly reorganizes from a disordered to an ordered state. The physics solution involves modeling control and order parameters to predict the threshold of transition. Adapted to the social sciences, this research would frame border porosity and state power as variables in a complex system. It seeks to identify the specific, measurable conditions—akin to critical points in physics—that act as forces to catalyze a systemic 'phase transition' from a globalized, open-border regime back to a world of resealed, hard territorial containers, providing a predictive model for geopolitical hardening.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Boundary Dynamics to Historical Sovereignty
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption of complete territorial sovereignty by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, the solution to the problem of rigid boundary models was the development of 'ecotone' theory and permeability metrics, focusing on fluxes and gradients rather than lines. Adapting this, the research would investigate historical borders as dynamic socio-political ecotones. The methodology would involve identifying key 'flows' (migration, trade, communication, legal jurisdiction) across specific historical border regions and developing metrics to map their intensity and permeability over time, thereby demonstrating that incomplete control and regulated porosity were constitutive features of pre-modern and early modern sovereignty, not anomalies.

ideas.title: Apply policy diffusion models to CR initiatives
ideas.description: Use political science models of policy diffusion to study the spread of voluntary CR initiatives across sectors.
ideas.sourceTitle: Strategy and Financial Policy in UK Small Firms

ideas.title: Applying Dark Matter Detection Methods to Identify Critical but Low-Visibility Network Nodes
ideas.description: This research idea inverts the assumption that a node's importance in a network is proportional to its visible activity or connectivity. Drawing an analogy from astrophysics, where dark matter is detected not by light but by its gravitational influence, we propose adapting dark matter detection methods to network analysis. The analogous solution involves inferring presence through indirect effects like gravitational lensing. Adapted to this context, the research would develop techniques to identify globally significant but dimly lit nodes (e.g., financial data hubs, key logistical chokepoints) by analyzing their 'gravitational' effects—such as distortions in data flow patterns, transaction settlement times, routing efficiencies, or systemic risk concentrations—rather than their direct, visible signatures. This allows for the mapping of a network's hidden critical architecture.

ideas.title: Use topic modeling on firm service portfolios
ideas.description: Apply Latent Dirichlet Allocation to firm service offerings to identify latent functional themes across cities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hierarchical Tendencies and Regional Patterns in the World City Network: A Global Urban Analysis of 234 Cities

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Analysis of Urban Light Networks Using Satellite Time-Series
ideas.description: This research adapts phylogenetic solution methods from evolutionary biology to analyze the historical evolution of the city-light metageography. The analogous problem—reconstructing evolutionary trees from genetic sequences over time—is solved using statistical models of character state evolution on aligned sequences. Here, multi-decadal global nighttime light data (e.g., from DMSP-OLS and VIIRS) is treated as aligned 'character state sequences' for geographic units. By applying adapted phylogenetic comparative methods (e.g., maximum likelihood models for 'trait' evolution of light intensity), the research will infer ancestral light configurations, identify key divergence points in the global urban network's growth, and phylogenetically classify types of urban expansion, distinguishing between shared, inherited growth patterns and independently evolved convergent developments in the planet's luminous landscape.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Resilience Theory to State Territorial Governance
ideas.description: This research seed expands by drawing an analogy from ecology, specifically resilience theory. In ecology, enforcing a single, homogeneous stable state on a complex ecosystem is now understood to reduce its resilience and increase vulnerability to collapse. The analogous solution is to manage for heterogeneity and adaptive capacity across multiple possible stable states. Adapted to political geography, this challenges the core assumption that internal state territorial and institutional homogenization is inevitable or optimal for stability. The research will explore cases where states have rejected full standardization, instead maintaining asymmetric or polycentric governance structures. By applying the resilience framework, it will analyze how such internal heterogeneity can function as a source of long-term state durability and adaptive capacity, offering an alternative model to the homogenizing nation-state paradigm.

ideas.title: Scale up to supranational governance bodies
ideas.description: Analyze how bodies like the IMF or EU shape national crisis responses within the framework.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Materialist Framework for Political Geography

ideas.title: Scale up parody to institutional mimicry
ideas.description: Examine large-scale, sustained mimicry of global institutions by NGOs or networks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Globalization in Question. The International Economy and the Possibilities of Governance

ideas.title: Contingent Containers: A Multi-Trajectory Framework for Modeling the Future of Territorial Control
ideas.description: This research systematically maps the competing drivers and conditions that could lead to divergent futures for state territorial control, ranging from strong resealing to persistent permeability or hybrid governance. Instead of presupposing a resurgence, it constructs multiple, evidence-based scenarios and identifies the key early-warning indicators that would signal a shift toward each trajectory. The approach rigorously tests the 'resealing' hypothesis against historical data, contemporary pressures, and explicit counter-scenarios, producing a probabilistic assessment of future pathways rather than a deterministic forecast. This makes the analysis robust to bias and capable of identifying which specific forces are ascendant under defined conditions.

ideas.title: Invert focus from city prestige to fan engagement metrics
ideas.description: Base expansion on direct measures of local fan passion rather than city status.
ideas.sourceTitle: A roster of world cities

ideas.title: Scale across policy domains beyond environment
ideas.description: Apply the sector-specific initiative model to social CR issues like labor practices or community investment.
ideas.sourceTitle: Strategy and Financial Policy in UK Small Firms

ideas.title: Scale down: study the household as a cellular unit with membrane dynamics
ideas.description: This research adapts the analytical framework of the cell membrane from cell biology to study the household as a mini-territorial unit. Just as a cell membrane provides selective permeability and regulates flows to maintain internal equilibrium, a household maintains borders (physical, digital, social) and sovereignty through regulated economic and social flows. The adapted idea is to map and analyze these household 'membrane dynamics'—identifying the mechanisms of inflow (income, groceries, information), outflow (waste, expenditures, commuters), and boundary control (locks, privacy settings, social rules)—to understand how the unit achieves stability, responds to external pressure, and defines its functional autonomy within the larger economic and social organism.

ideas.title: Apply the Coase Theorem with transaction cost analysis to territorial sovereignty disputes
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from environmental economics. The analogous problem is the management of common-pool resources or transboundary pollution, where the Coase Theorem is used to frame conflicts over usage rights. The analogous solution involves analyzing how high transaction costs prevent efficient bargaining over clearly defined but initially misallocated property rights, leading to persistent inefficiency. Adapted to territorial sovereignty, the idea models state territoriality as a bundle of property rights subject to transaction costs. It specifically applies the Coase Theorem framework to sovereignty disputes, treating them as bargaining failures. The research would identify, categorize, and measure the real-world transaction costs—such as the costs of verifying compliance, the political costs of concession, the costs of enforcing agreements, and informational asymmetries—that block mutually beneficial settlements over territory and shared resources. The outcome is a transaction-cost-based theory explaining the persistence of territorial conflict and a framework for designing institutions (e.g., treaties, joint authorities, verification mechanisms) to mitigate these costs and facilitate cooperation.

ideas.title: Scale to virtual spaces as new territories
ideas.description: Map how cyber domains and platforms replicate state-like territorial logics.
ideas.sourceTitle: The state as container: territoriality in the modern world-system

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Comparative Methods to Model the Evolution of Territorial Homogenization
ideas.description: This research adapts a core solution from evolutionary biology—phylogenetic comparative methods (PCMs)—to the historical GIS study of territorial homogenization. In biology, PCMs use statistical models on evolutionary trees to control for shared ancestry when testing hypotheses about adaptive evolution. Adapted here, the method involves constructing a network or 'phylogeny' of states based on historical lineage, colonial inheritance, or legal tradition. GIS is then used to quantify spatial standardization metrics (administrative geometry, infrastructure grids, etc.) for each state. By applying PCMs to this spatial-historical dataset, the research can statistically disentangle whether territorial homogenization in a given region is primarily a legacy of a shared institutional ancestor (like the Roman Empire or Napoleonic Code) or an independent, convergent outcome of modern state-building, thereby refining theories of state-driven spatial standardization.

ideas.title: Analyze pre-2008 neoliberal policy formation historically
ideas.description: Trace how domestic policy formation historically constituted global neoliberal parameters before the 2008 crisis.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Materialist Framework for Political Geography

ideas.title: Institutional Economics of Non-Territorial Sovereignty: Modeling Bundles of Rights in Data, Law, and Identity
ideas.description: This research synthesizes institutional economics with the study of non-territorial sovereignty to develop a comparative theory of sovereign rights allocation. It models the core attributes of data, legal, and identity-based sovereignty as discrete, tradable 'bundles of rights,' analogous to property rights in land, which are created, exchanged, partitioned, and enforced subject to distinct transaction costs. This framework allows for the analysis of how different institutional arrangements (e.g., tech platforms, diasporic assemblies, international tribunals) emerge to minimize these costs and govern non-territorial spaces, providing a common metric to explain the stability, conflict, and hybridity observed across all forms of sovereignty.

ideas.title: Scale down: CR in micro-firms and sole traders
ideas.description: Study corporate responsibility practices and policy impacts on very small businesses, not just small firms.
ideas.sourceTitle: Strategy and Financial Policy in UK Small Firms

ideas.title: Sovereign Capacity: A Power-Analytic Framework for Non-Territorial Authority
ideas.description: This research moves beyond cataloging non-territorial sovereignty to develop a diagnostic framework for measuring 'sovereign capacity'—the ability to make an ultimate authority claim stick. It identifies key power resources (e.g., control over critical data flows, exclusive legal adjudication, mobilization of identity-based loyalty) and develops metrics to assess their effectiveness in performing core sovereign functions like security provision, resource generation, and population governance. The approach enables systematic, comparative case studies between non-territorial entities (e.g., platform states, diasporic nations, autonomous religious orders) and traditional states, evaluating their resilience, points of failure, and potential for conflict or hybrid integration within the international system.

ideas.title: Modeling territorial leakage in world-systems using epidemiological compartmental frameworks
ideas.description: This research adapts epidemiological compartmental modeling to assess territoriality's role in future world-system crises. The analogous problem from epidemiology involves modeling how population subgroups (compartments) and flows between them (e.g., infection, recovery) determine systemic outbreak dynamics, with particular focus on how 'leakage' (like imperfect immunity) alters stability thresholds. The adapted solution treats world-system territories as compartments (e.g., Strong Container, Leaking Container, Crisis State) and models the flows of capital, resources, and political control between them. By applying differential equations similar to SIR models, we can simulate how increasing 'leakage' from territorial containers—through failed sovereignty, capital flight, or porous borders—accelerates transitions from accumulation to crisis phases, identifying critical leakage rates that trigger systemic failure.

ideas.title: Triangulating Territorial Narratives: A Multi-Perspective Computational Model of Contested Sovereignty
ideas.description: This research system moves beyond a single-state lens to model territorial contestation as a dynamic narrative struggle. It computationally analyzes and contrasts three parallel streams: official state discourse, counter-narratives from sub-state actors, and empirical ground-truth data on control. By aligning these corpora, the system identifies narrative divergence points, strategic framing shifts, and measures the coherence between claimed and de facto authority. This triangulation provides a robust, adversarial framework for understanding sovereignty not as a monolithic claim but as a negotiated and contested information space.

ideas.title: Modeling Adversarial Opacity: A Dynamic Systems Approach to Latent Network Influence
ideas.description: This research moves beyond static identification of 'dark' nodes to model how strategic actors dynamically manage their visibility and functional footprint within global networks. It develops a sensing and modeling framework that infers latent influence not from a node's own signals, but from the distortions, adaptations, and systemic behaviors it induces in the observable network around it. The approach treats opacity as an adversarial variable, using recursive feedback between probe actions and network adaptations to predict where critical functions might migrate, thereby creating a persistent map of influence that remains valid even as surface-level signatures change.

ideas.title: Planetary Biosphere Control Theory: Applying Cybernetic Principles to Global System Regulation
ideas.description: This idea treats the planetary biosphere as the ultimate territorial unit facing global crises, analogous to a single, complex container system. Drawing from Control Systems Engineering and Cybernetics, it adapts the solution of applying control theory to manage engineered systems. The analogous solution involves using sensors, feedback loops, and actuators to maintain system stability. Adapted to this context, the research would frame the Earth as a system where key state variables (climate metrics, biodiversity, biogeochemical flows) are the outputs, and human industrial, agricultural, and economic processes are the controllable inputs. The goal is to develop a cybernetic framework for 'planetary management,' identifying requisite feedback mechanisms and designing institutional and technological 'controllers' capable of regulating inputs to keep the biosphere within a predefined safe and stable operating space, thus scaling governance to the container level.
